q: Which effect of electric current is used in an electric heater?
sol: Electric heaters convert electrical energy into heat energy through resistance.

q: The material used for the filament of an incandescent bulb is:
sol: Tungsten has a very high melting point, preventing it from melting at high temperatures.

q: Who discovered the magnetic effect of electric current?
sol: Oersted noticed a compass needle deflect near a wire carrying current in 1820.

q: A safety device that breaks the circuit when excessive current flows is:
sol: A fuse melts when current exceeds a safe limit, protecting the appliances.

q: The core of an electromagnet is usually made of:
sol: Soft iron is easily magnetized and demagnetized, making it ideal for electromagnets.

q: Which of these works on the magnetic effect of current?
sol: Electric bells use an electromagnet to pull an iron strip to strike the gong.

q: What happens to a compass needle kept near a current-carrying wire?
sol: The magnetic field produced by the current interacts with the compass magnet.

q: The heating effect of current depends on:
sol: According to Joule's law, Heat (H) = I²RT.

q: The heating element of an electric iron is made of:
sol: Nichrome is an alloy with high resistance and high melting point.

q: MCB stands for:
sol: MCBs are modern switches that automatically turn off during overloads.

q: An electromagnet is a:
sol: It only acts as a magnet as long as electric current flows through it.

q: Increasing the number of turns in a coil ________ the strength of the electromagnet.
sol: More turns create a stronger concentrated magnetic field.

q: The short-circuiting occurs when:
o: Live and neutral wires touch directly
sol: Direct contact leads to a sudden huge flow of current.

q: Which property is essential for a fuse wire?
sol: It must melt quickly if the current becomes dangerously high.

q: Electric cranes use ________ to lift heavy iron scrap.
sol: Electromagnets allow the crane to pick up and drop metal easily by switching current.

q: The magnetic field around a straight current-carrying wire is in the shape of:
sol: The magnetic field lines encircle the wire.

q: If the direction of current is reversed, the magnetic field direction:
sol: Magnetic field direction is directly linked to the direction of current flow.

q: The fuse wire is usually an alloy of:
sol: The tin-lead alloy has the appropriate low melting point.

q: A solenoid acts like a ________ when current passes through it.
sol: A long coil of wire (solenoid) creates a magnetic field similar to a bar magnet.

q: Which of the following does not use the heating effect of current?
sol: Electric fans primarily use the magnetic effect to drive a motor.

q: ISI mark on electrical appliances ensures:
sol: ISI mark signifies that the product meets the safety standards set by the Bureau of Indian Standards.

q: The magnetic effect of current can be observed in:
sol: Only conductors carrying electric current produce a magnetic field.

q: When current flows through a wire, it behaves like a:
sol: This is the fundamental definition of the magnetic effect of current.

q: A glowing bulb becomes hot due to:
sol: Resistance in the filament produces heat as a byproduct of light.

q: Which appliance works on both heating and magnetic effects?
sol: A hair dryer has a heating element (heat) and a motor/fan (magnetic).

q: A 'fuse' is always connected in ________ with the appliance.
sol: Series connection ensures all current passes through the fuse before the appliance.

q: To make a stronger electromagnet, we can:
sol: Higher current intensity generates a stronger magnetic field.

q: Which device uses a permanent magnet and an electromagnet?
sol: Loudspeakers use the interaction between both to produce sound vibrations.

q: The space around a magnet where its influence is felt is called:
sol: The magnetic field is the region where magnetic forces act.

q: Electric current is measured in:
sol: Amperes (A) is the SI unit of electric current.

q: Resistance of a wire is ________ proportional to its thickness.
sol: Thicker wires have lower resistance, allowing current to flow more easily.

q: Which of these is a poor conductor of electricity?
sol: Pure distilled water lacks ions to conduct electricity.

q: A fuse is a ________ link in an electrical circuit.
sol: It is designed to be the first part to break to save others.

q: The amount of heat produced in a wire depends on its:
sol: Material, length, and cross-section all determine the total resistance.

q: What protects our house from lightning?
sol: Lightning conductors provide a low-resistance path for lightning to reach the ground.

q: If a circuit is 'open', the current:
sol: An open circuit has a gap that prevents the flow of electrons.

q: Which effect is used in electroplating?
sol: Electroplating uses electricity to cause a chemical change in a solution.

q: The coil of wire in an electric heater is called:
sol: The 'element' is the part that gets red hot and provides heat.

q: Magnetic field lines always start from:
sol: By convention, magnetic field lines emerge from the North pole.

q: An electric fuse works on the:
sol: It works on the principle that current produces heat which can melt the wire.

q: The magnetic field inside a solenoid is:
sol: Inside the solenoid, field lines are straight and parallel, indicating a uniform field.

q: When the strength of current is increased, the deflection in the compass:
sol: Stronger current creates a stronger magnetic field, pushing the needle harder.

q: Which of these is used to detect the presence of current?
sol: A galvanometer is a sensitive instrument that detects small currents via magnetic deflection.

q: A high resistance wire produces ________ heat than a low resistance wire.
sol: More resistance results in more collisions and more heat (H=I²RT).

q: A fuse is usually placed in which wire?
sol: Placing it in the live wire cuts off the high-voltage supply entirely if it melts.

q: Which of the following is an alloy used in heating elements?
sol: Nichrome (Nickel + Chromium) is the standard for heating elements.

q: A group of cells connected together is called a:
sol: A battery is two or more cells combined to provide higher voltage.

q: The magnetic effect of current is used in which medical technology?
sol: MRI stands for Magnetic Resonance Imaging.

q: What is the unit of resistance?
sol: The Ohm (Ω) is the unit named after Georg Simon Ohm.

q: Current is the flow of:
sol: Electric current in a wire is the directed movement of negative electrons.

q: Which of these will not be attracted by an electromagnet?
sol: Copper is a non-magnetic material.

q: The purpose of 'earthing' is to:
sol: Earthing transfers leakage current safely to the ground.

q: An electric bell contains an:
sol: The electromagnet attracts the armature to ring the bell.

q: In a battery, the longer line in the symbol represents:
sol: The long thin line is positive; the short thick line is negative.

q: Which of the following generates a lot of heat?
sol: Nichrome has significantly higher resistance compared to the others.

q: A compact fluorescent lamp (CFL) is better than a bulb because it:
sol: CFLs convert more energy to light rather than wasting it as heat.

q: Who made the first electric battery?
sol: The 'Voltaic Pile' was the first chemical battery.

q: Heating effect is unwanted in which device?
sol: In computers, heat is a harmful byproduct that requires cooling fans.

q: In an electric bell, the circuit is broken by the:
sol: When the armature moves, it loses contact with the screw, breaking the circuit.

q: If we wrap a wire around a plastic pen and pass current, will it be a magnet?
sol: A current-carrying coil creates a magnetic field regardless of the core, though a plastic core won't amplify it.

q: What is the standard voltage of domestic power supply in India?
sol: India uses a 220-240V AC supply at 50Hz.

q: An electromagnet is used in 'Maglev' trains for:
sol: Maglev stands for Magnetic Levitation.

q: Which of these is a safety precaution for electricity?
sol: A fuse prevents fires caused by overcurrent.

q: The soft iron inside an electromagnet helps to:
sol: It concentrates the magnetic flux lines.

q: Which wire is generally used for making the connecting wires in a circuit?
sol: Copper has very low resistance and is inexpensive.

q: The property of 'superconductivity' means a material has:
sol: Superconductors allow current to flow without any loss of energy as heat.

q: Does a current-carrying wire attract a plastic bead?
o: Only if the bead is wet
sol: Magnetism only affects magnetic materials (iron, cobalt, nickel).

q: A heater wire glowing red is an example of:
sol: Incandescence is the emission of light due to high temperature.

q: What happens if you increase the voltage in a circuit?
sol: According to Ohm's Law (V=IR), increasing V increases I if R is constant.

q: Which device is used to open or close a circuit?
sol: A switch acts as a bridge that can be connected or disconnected.

q: Is the magnetic field of a bar magnet stronger at the poles or center?
sol: Magnetic lines of force are most crowded at the poles.

q: Which material has the highest electrical conductivity?
sol: Silver is the best conductor, but copper is used more often due to cost.

q: An electric iron consumes electricity and produces:
sol: It utilizes the heating effect of current.

q: A magnetic compass always points towards:
sol: It aligns with the Earth's magnetic field.

q: In a parallel circuit, if one bulb fuses:
sol: Parallel circuits provide independent paths for each component.

q: The symbol for 'A' in an electric circuit diagram represents:
sol: A circle with an 'A' inside is the standard symbol for an ammeter.

q: Which of these is not a magnetic material?
sol: Aluminum is paramagnetic but not ferromagnetic (it's not 'magnetic' in daily terms).

q: Which wire should never be used as a fuse?
sol: Thick copper has a high melting point and won't melt during a surge, risking a fire.

q: An electromagnet used in a scrap yard is attached to a:
sol: Cranes lift and move the magnetic load.

q: What creates the sound in an electric bell?
sol: Mechanical impact produces the sound waves.

q: As the resistance increases, the current ________.
sol: Resistance opposes the flow of current.

q: Magnetic field lines ________ each other.
sol: If they crossed, the compass would point in two directions at once, which is impossible.

q: The magnetic effect of current is also called:
sol: It describes the relationship between electricity and magnetism.

q: A galvanometer can show:
sol: The needle deflects to different sides based on current direction.

q: Electric power is measured in:
sol: Watt (W) is the unit of power (Energy per second).

q: Domestic wiring is done in:
sol: Parallel wiring ensures constant voltage and independent operation of devices.

q: Which device converts mechanical energy into electrical energy?
sol: Generators use the inverse effect (electromagnetic induction).

q: What determines the polarity of an electromagnet?
sol: Changing current direction swaps the North and South poles.

q: A cell is a device that converts ________ energy to electrical energy.
sol: Chemical reactions inside the cell push electrons through the circuit.

q: Which of these is a magnetic effect application?
sol: Maglev trains use powerful magnets to float above tracks.

q: To prevent overheating, appliances often use a:
sol: A thermostat automatically switches off the current when a set temperature is reached.

q: Which pole of a compass needle is usually painted red?
sol: The North-seeking pole is usually highlighted.

q: Why is a solenoid more effective than a single wire loop?
sol: The magnetic fields of each loop add up together.

q: What happens if a fuse is bypassed using a copper wire?
sol: Without a fuse, wires can melt and catch fire during a fault.

q: The heating effect was first studied quantitatively by:
sol: Joule's Law of heating is named after him.

q: Which of these is used as an insulator?
sol: Plastic does not allow current to pass through it.

q: When current stops flowing, an electromagnet:
sol: It is a temporary magnet; magnetism vanishes with the current.

q: A thick wire has ________ resistance than a thin wire of the same material.
sol: Think of it like a wider road allowing more traffic (current) to flow.

q: The strength of a magnetic field is measured in:
sol: Tesla (T) is the SI unit for magnetic field strength.

q: Electrical energy is sold in units of:
sol: 1 unit on your electricity bill is equal to 1 kWh.
